headline: Riley is learning the codebase quickly, but decision-making and ownership boundaries are still unclear, so he’s defaulting to notes instead of asking in real time.
summary_bullets: Riley can trace request paths, but he’s missing the historical context behind tradeoffs and how decisions are actually made.
When priorities conflict, he doesn’t know who owns each domain, which pushes him toward “keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.”
He wants incident-ready escalation norms and live handoff details that the runbook doesn’t fully cover.
understanding_paragraph: Riley is progressing fast through service/codebase orientation, but the missing piece is the human decision layer: who decides, why tradeoffs were made, and who’s on point when priorities collide. He’s actively trying not to interrupt, which turns “questions” into deferred notes rather than timely context-seeking. The onboarding opportunity is to convert his questions into a clear escalation-vs-batch guide plus structured senior touchpoints.

axes.meaning: Stress shows up as friction from unclear incident/ownership expectations; weak recovery signal, worth addressing early.
Mild disengagement signal: he’s trying to contribute, but uncertainty is dampening confidence more than motivation.
Strong clarity gap: decision-making, ownership boundaries, and escalation-vs-batch norms are not yet reliable.
No clear growth signal yet; he’s asking for calibration support, but learning trajectory isn’t established.

brutal_truth_employee: Riley said, “I am comfortable reading the services, but I am less sure how decisions are really made here.” He also shared, “I am trying not to interrupt people, so sometimes I keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking,” which means key context is getting delayed until later.
brutal_truth_manager: Riley gave a concrete friction moment: “keep questions in a notes doc instead of asking.” The next time, deepen by turning that into an explicit “in-the-moment” protocol—what to ask, who to ask, and when—rather than letting it stay as a habit description.

next_actions.action: Create and send Riley a one-page “escalate vs batch” guide with 3 examples and named domain owners for conflicts.
Schedule a 30-minute weekly architecture calibration session for Riley with a senior owner; set a standing agenda: tradeoff history + decision criteria.
Confirm Riley used the guide in the last incident review: ask which question he would have deferred to notes and what changed.

watch_for: Within two weeks, if Riley still says he “keeps questions in a notes doc,” treat it as the guide not being actionable enough.
Before next 1:1, if Riley can name the “on point” owner for each domain during a conflict, clarity is improving.
